I believe quick commerce will continue to thrive until local sellers start embracing hyper-local deliveries. Once general trade stores adopt fast delivery models, it could pose a serious challenge to both e-commerce and quick commerce platforms. Take restaurants, for example – if they handle their own deliveries, there's less reliance on services like Zomato and Swiggy. This shift could reshape the landscape, giving local businesses more control and potentially more profit. Just my two cents!

Here is my hypothesis how these apps will kill everything, making all the goods we consume absolutely expensive in the future.

I believe that the rise of certain delivery apps could ultimately lead to significantly higher prices for everyday goods, similar to the disruption caused by JIO in the telecom sector, which eventually resulted in price hikes.

THE PLAY BOOK:

- Funding: These companies raise substantial capital from investors to offer products at lower prices initially.

- Customer Acquisition: They attract customers with competitive pricing and faster service.

- Impact on Local Businesses: As consumers flock to these platforms, local kirana stores struggle to compete and may eventually shut down.

- Monopoly and Pricing Power: Once local stores are gone, consumers will have no choice but to rely on these online services, giving the apps control over pricing and fees. (Similar to the situation in US, where Amazon is dominant)

- Future Cost Increases: In 5 to 10 years, you could find yourself paying 3 to 10 times more for the same goods.

Before placing your next order through these apps, consider the following:

"Do I really need this in 10 minutes?"
"Could I support your local kirana store instead, who may offer the same price?""
"Should I order through an APP while I keep watching TV or working tierlessly, or should I go down take a stroll in the neighborhood".

The  local Kirana store, the bajiwala on the street, the vendor all of them is what makes India unique, they play a vital roll of a community, they provide employment to many.

The 10 min delivery is just BS. I work at Zepto as delivery rider full time and I have understood a lot abt how customers get their products. Firstly, some customers get wrong products for which they have not ordered (the packing staff at Zepto Hub is responsible for this). Second, the 10 min delivery will only apply if the Zepto Hub is near your location. As a general rule, zepto riders get orders within the range of 3-4 km, so if you are located near the hub then you may even get the order in under 10 min, conversely it may take 20-30 min for a customer who’s located at a 3-4 km range from the hub. Third, the zepto riders mostly work for order incentives which mean surely they will deliver the product to you but expect that they will also cut traffic signals, have to pay fine for it, may get into an accident, etc just to deliver your item. Never trust the 10 min delivery scheme such businesses promote. It’s nothing but fishy

While the business-economics points you mentioned are interesting, I’m honestly just watching this during a tea break 😅 Disclaimer: I’m not employed by Swiggy or Blinkit.
One place where you did go off track, and this actually falls squarely in my work domain as a Creative Director with 15+ years of experience:

1. InstaMart is easy to pronounce. Confusing it with Instagram feels like a "you" 😜 problem, not a branding one. Both Blinkit and InstaMart follow a simple, clean syllable structure that works well for Indian users.

2. UI wise , I partly agree. Swiggy’s main app "looks" busy, but it isn’t poorly designed. It’s very deliberately organised. This style works for Indian consumers, where more visible information often equals more trust. Whoever planned Swiggy’s UI clearly took cues from Amazon’s app structure, blended with Indian web UI design sensibilities where density beats minimalism.

3. The colour critique was a bit off. Swiggy has always leaned orange, and I think 🤔 around the InstaMart launch they introduced that blue neon accent (using layman terms here). Blinkit, Bistro, and Zomato, on the other hand, feel visually scattered. If three apps come from the same parent brand, keeping a tighter colour system would’ve made more sense than forcing a “new app UI color experience” every time.

4. This may sound harsh, but  your design analysis came more from a management POV the usual “copy what works” argument. Yes, Swiggy borrowed certain layout frameworks (Amazon influence is obvious), but the Swiggy mother app’s homepage is still quite unique . Put Blinkit and Swiggy side by side and it’s clear... these are two products with distinct identities, not carbon copies wearing different logos.

Rest of the economic stuff, I don’t really have the technical knowledge... but it sounds interesting enough to casually repeat when I’m talking to people who do 😜

So I almost always used Swiggy until 2-3 years ago since it’s a Bangalore based company and u gotta support the local businesses and after transacting for more than a Lakh on Swiggy, I cancelled an order 2-3 minutes after ordering since I changed my mind (this is the first time this happened). For this Swiggy charged me the full order amount 1000 bucks as a cancelation fee which I didn’t wanna pay. Because of this I couldn’t use Swiggy anymore and with extension instamart even if it had something that blinkit didn’t (in the past, now there’s no comparison between the 2). But as I moved to Zomato for food ordering because of the cancellation fee, I also lost access to instamart and shifted to blinkit and Zepto (using them parallelly) . It’s honestly sad that I got banned and can’t support a Bangalore based business but with the way Zomato and Swiggy have moved in the opposite directions, I couldn’t be happier using Zomato and by extension blinkit now, since the customer service is better and nothing like this has happened w Zomato even when I’ve cancelled orders which are out for delivery, because they understand their customer base better. But the point is that this super app model, which has shown to not work in India, is going to isolate customers by trying to bring them in the Swiggy ecosystem (similar to how apple does it) but the second something goes wrong between the customer and Swiggy then they loose access to all revenue streams from the customer and the customer will not want to or be able to touch any of the products in the Swiggy ecosystem

Hey Vibhanshu, 
First things first I love watching your videos.
I was also analysing the Financial statements of Qcom companies because I am interested to see what lies in future for these Companies.

First thing first, Instamart is bleeding right now because their Marketing spends, Capex (expansion) and may be inefficiency (Can't say for sure as they never clearly said in result that WE ARE INEFFICIENT THAN OUR PEERS) (other details as mentioned in their Financials) (but still blinkit is more popular in terms of brand awareness.  Wtf is heading marketing for swiggy and spending so much money should be held accountable)

Speaking of GOV and NOV, Blinkit sells wayy more High Ticket, High Priced, High margin products like iphones, headphones, Electronics etc. heck even 190 litre fridges. And electronics offer very high margins. And Since they are now shifting to inventory led model they are basically trying to become Dmart or Reliance for Online Grocery which gives them full quality control (which increases customer trust in long run resulting in higher orders) and also price control since they can have higher bargaining power while placing orders in Bulk. They will be keeping whole 100% of the margins instead of 20-30% like Instamart. Which overall impacts results a lot.

Here's an example for Non Finance, Non Geek folks: 
Product A: COGS: 800
MRP: 2500
Discount: 30% (-750)
Revenue recieved: 1750
Overheads, Delivery fees, Salary etc all consists of around say (48.5%) Remaining is 900. So keeping it simple lets assume 100 is Net profit here so Swiggy, since it runs on commission based model (market place model will keep 30-40% of revenue at max, say 40% here to maximise our result) NP: 40, Blinkit since it is wholesaler, relailer, Platform etc etc. It will take everything 100 (Plus platform fees and all but let's keep things simple)
Hence Blinkit will anyday have much much more NP than Instamart. (Assuming both are operating at same efficiency).
It's the same case of Dmart and XYZ mall where all brand outlets are independent shops.

Now coming to App.
Here, I respectfully disagree with you.
I think only reason Instamart is able to Achieve whatever it has is because of it being integrated in main Swiggy app.  I use Instamart because it let's me order from one app and I do not like installing 100 apps on phone so whenever I need food or grocery I just go to Swiggy app (They also launched separate Instamart app BTW). They also now have improved the UI, UX a lot, giving you options in big and bold like Food, Dineout, Instamart Genie etc. (They offer separate app for each btw).
Brand Color and Brand image is something that they need to work and yes Sometimes their app just doesn't feels as polished as Blinkit. (But I do not like blinkit either in terms of smoothness. I think in that area Big Basket is the best).

So if you read this far, (Just Say BINGO if you did) Congratulations you got nothing you didn't already know. But I just tried sharing my view that 80% of issue lies in their business model which isn't sustainable at all (Even worse model that theirs was of Dunzo which failed). And Remaining 20% lies in marketing and efficiency (IDK where are they spending their million dollar Marketing Budget)
One out of context thing but I generally order from Swiggy because their platform allows ordering from PC while Zomato is Stupid enough to not let me do so. 

Now what's the solution according to Some World Renowned Harvard Prodigy whom you've never head of:
1. Shift towards Inventory led Model.
2. Better prediction models. This will reduce wastage of Perishables.
3. Start analysing Searches and work on them. (Suppose if 100,000  people searched iphone 17 on Swiggy then just make it available)
4. Start Selling High Margin, High Ticket items (Electronics, Apparels etc) Basically moving from necessity to Luxury products as people are ready to pay more there and margins are thicker too.
5. Become Reliance Retail or Dmart or Big Bazaar (Inhouse Tiered brands with Packaging and Branding getting better with price Say Golden harvest almonds vs Karmiq almonds both are almonds but with 30% difference in price and may be 10% better quality but Packaging and Branding gets much premium.) 

So yeah these were my 2 paise (2 cents). If anyone from company is reading this and want to implement then please do. I love ordering things all from 1 app.
Thanks for Reading!

Yesterday I realised something painful.

A customer came to my store, selected a product, checked it physically, asked questions, took time deciding… and then opened Amazon in front of me.

The same product was available there at an extra discount with delivery to their home.

The customer left.

People talk about “convenience” and “discounts,” but nobody talks about what’s happening to local stores. Small retailers spend on rent, staff, inventory, electricity, and still become just a free display showroom for big platforms.

I honestly don’t know whether quick commerce and ecommerce are even profitable long term… but one thing is clear — they are damaging thousands of local businesses every single day.

Support local stores whenever you can.
A market survives because of small businesses, not only giant apps.
